labels.description: A deep groove separating the frontal lobe from the parietal lobe.
The portion of the cerebral cortex on the medial surface surrounding the central sulcus.
The terminal portion of the cingulate sulcus that curves upward to the superior border of the hemisphere.
A C-shaped bundle of white matter fibers that acts as the major output tract of the hippocampus.
The portion of the superior parietal lobule on the medial surface, anterior to the cuneus.
A wedge-shaped section of the occipital lobe on the medial surface.
A deep groove marking the boundary between the parietal and occipital lobes.
A small endocrine gland in the brain that produces melatonin.
The narrow part of the cingulate gyrus that connects it with the parahippocampal gyrus.
An anatomical landmark located at the caudal end of the medial surface of the brain, housing the primary visual cortex.
A tongue-shaped gyrus of the occipital lobe located between the calcarine and collateral sulci.
The 'little brain' located at the back of the skull, vital for motor control and coordination.
The long, thin, tubular bundle of nervous tissue that extends from the brainstem.
The lowest part of the brainstem, responsible for autonomic functions like breathing and heart rate.
The part of the brainstem that links the medulla oblongata and the thalamus.
The topmost part of the brainstem, involved in visual and auditory processing.
The pathway for visual information from the eyes to the brain, where fibers partially cross.
A region of the forebrain below the thalamus that coordinates the autonomic nervous system.
A large mass of gray matter relaying sensory signals to the cerebral cortex.
The caudal part of the forebrain, containing the thalamus and hypothalamus.
A thin sheet of gray matter forming the anterior wall of the third ventricle.
A small gyrus on the medial surface of the hemisphere, anterior to the lamina terminalis.
A bundle of nerve fibers connecting the two temporal lobes.
The anterior-inferior part of the corpus callosum.
The anterior bend of the corpus callosum.
The thick posterior part of the corpus callosum.
The central part of the corpus callosum between the genu and splenium.
labels.etymology: Old English 'bodig' (main part of a structure).

labels.description: The large band of neural fibers connecting the two brain hemispheres.
The uppermost convolution of the frontal lobe.
The groove separating the cingulate gyrus from the frontal and parietal lobes.
A curved gyrus covering the corpus callosum, part of the limbic system.
A thin membrane separating the left and right lateral ventricles.
A prominent groove separating the frontal lobe from the parietal lobe and the primary motor cortex from the primary somatosensory cortex.
The primary somatosensory cortex, responsible for processing touch and proprioception.
A groove parallel to the central sulcus that forms the posterior boundary of the postcentral gyrus.
A region involved in spatial orientation and the integration of sensory input.
A horizontal groove in the parietal lobe that divides the superior and inferior parietal lobules.
A cortical region involved in language, mathematical operations, and complex sensory integration.
Part of the inferior parietal lobule involved in phonological processing and emotional responses.
Part of the inferior parietal lobule involved in language, number processing, and memory retrieval.
Convolutions of the occipital lobe primarily responsible for visual processing.
The most posterior point of the occipital lobe.
An indentation on the inferolateral border of the hemisphere that serves as a landmark for the boundary between the temporal and occipital lobes.
A large structure of the hindbrain that controls fine motor skills and balance.
The pathway for messages sent between the brain and the body.
The base of the brainstem; controls heartbeat and breathing.
Relays information from the cerebellum to the rest of the brain.
The lowest of the three temporal gyri, involved in high-level visual processing and object recognition.
The groove separating the middle and inferior temporal gyri.
Involved in recognition of faces and word meaning during reading.
The groove separating the superior and middle temporal gyri.
Contains the primary auditory cortex, responsible for processing sounds.
The most anterior portion of the temporal lobe.
The lateral sulcus that divides the temporal lobe from the frontal and parietal lobes.
Gyri on the inferior surface of the frontal lobe, involved in emotion and decision-making.
The most anterior part of the frontal lobe.
The inferior-most part of the inferior frontal gyrus.
The triangular-shaped part of the inferior frontal gyrus, part of Broca's area.
The posterior part of the inferior frontal gyrus, part of Broca's area.
The lowest gyrus of the frontal lobe, containing Broca's area in the dominant hemisphere.
The groove separating the middle and inferior frontal gyri.
A gyrus between the superior and inferior frontal sulci, involved in higher-level executive functions.
The groove separating the superior and middle frontal gyri.
The uppermost gyrus of the frontal lobe, involved in self-awareness and laughter.
A groove parallel to the central sulcus that forms the anterior boundary of the precentral gyrus.
The primary motor cortex, responsible for the execution of voluntary movements.
The most anterior portion of the frontal lobes.
Structures involved in the sense of smell, located on the inferior surface of the frontal lobe.
An area at the base of the brain where numerous small blood vessels enter.
The region of the brain responsible for hormone production and autonomic control.
Small round structures on the undersurface of the hypothalamus involved in recollective memory.
The most superior part of the brainstem, relaying sensory and motor information.
The most posterior point of the occipital lobe.
The deep groove that separates the two cerebral hemispheres.
A gyrus on the basal surface of the temporal and occipital lobes, critical for face and object recognition.
A groove on the lateral/inferior surface of the temporal lobe.
A gyrus on the inferior surface of the temporal lobe involved in visual processing.
A grey matter cortical region of the brain that surrounds the hippocampus.
The hook-shaped anterior end of the parahippocampal gyrus.
A groove on the inferior surface of the temporal lobe separating the fusiform and parahippocampal gyri.
The anterior-most part of the temporal lobe.
Gyri located on the inferior surface of the frontal lobe, above the eye orbits.
A groove on the inferior surface of the frontal lobe that houses the olfactory bulb.
A straight gyrus on the medial-inferior surface of the frontal lobe.
The large cleft separating the left and right hemispheres.
The groove anterior to the primary motor cortex.
The primary motor cortex of the brain.
The major groove separating the frontal and parietal lobes.
The primary somatosensory cortex.
The groove posterior to the primary somatosensory cortex.
The upper part of the parietal lobe involved in spatial awareness.
The lower part of the parietal lobe involved in complex sensory integration.
The groove dividing the superior and inferior parietal lobules.
The posterior-most tip of the brain.
A gyrus on the lateral surface of the frontal lobe.
The groove separating the superior and middle frontal gyri.
The uppermost gyrus of the frontal lobe.
A large mass of gray matter in the dorsal part of the diencephalon that relays sensory signals to the cerebral cortex.
The most superior portion of the brainstem, involved in visual and auditory reflexes.
The middle part of the brainstem that serves as a bridge between the medulla and the midbrain.
The lowest part of the brainstem, continuous with the spinal cord, containing vital autonomic centers.
The long, thin tubular structure made of nervous tissue that extends from the medulla.
The X-shaped structure formed by the crossing of the optic nerves.
The continuation of the optic nerve fibers from the optic chiasm to the lateral geniculate nucleus.
A depression on the ventral surface of the midbrain between the two cerebral peduncles.
Large bundles of nerve fibers on the ventral midbrain that carry motor tracts.
The largest of the three peduncles, connecting the pons to the cerebellum.
The junction between the cerebellum, pons, and medulla; a common site for acoustic neuromas.
A prominent oval swelling on the medulla involved in motor coordination.
Paired white matter structures of the medulla containing motor fibers of the corticospinal tract.
The area in the lower medulla where motor fibers cross the midline.
labels.etymology: Latin 'decussare' (to cross in an X shape).

labels.description: A motor nerve that controls the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles.
The nerve responsible for coordinating tongue movements.
The longest cranial nerve, providing parasympathetic control to the heart, lungs, and digestive tract.
A nerve involved in taste, swallowing, and the gag reflex.
The nerve responsible for the senses of hearing and balance.
The nerve controlling the muscles of facial expression.
The motor nerve that controls the lateral rectus muscle of the eye.
The largest cranial nerve, responsible for facial sensation and chewing.
labels.etymology: Latin 'trigeminus' (born three at once/triplets), for its three branches.

labels.description: A motor nerve that innervates the superior oblique muscle of the eye.
The nerve responsible for most eye movements and pupil constriction.
The sensory nerve that transmits visual information from the retina to the brain.
A bridge of white matter fibers connecting the optic tract and the superior colliculus.
A relay center in the thalamus for the visual pathway.
labels.etymology: Latin 'genu' (knee), referring to its bent shape.

labels.description: A part of the auditory thalamus that represents the thalamic relay between the inferior colliculus and the auditory cortex.
A bundle of auditory fibers connecting the inferior colliculus to the medial geniculate body.
The only cranial nerve to emerge from the dorsal aspect of the brainstem.
An elevation in the rhomboid fossa formed by the fibers of the facial nerve looping over the abducens nucleus.
A slight elevation in the floor of the fourth ventricle over the hypoglossal nucleus.
A small triangular area in the floor of the fourth ventricle overlying the dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus nerve.
The lateral portion of the dorsal column, carrying sensory information from the upper body.
The medial portion of the dorsal column, carrying sensory information from the lower body.
A medullary nucleus that receives input from the fasciculus gracilis.
A medullary nucleus that receives input from the fasciculus cuneatus.
The point in the medulla where the fourth ventricle narrows to become the central canal.
The primary output tract of the cerebellum to the midbrain.
A thin layer of white matter stretching between the superior cerebellar peduncles.
Paired structures on the dorsal midbrain involved in auditory processing.
Paired structures on the dorsal midbrain involved in visual reflexes.
A relay center in the thalamus for the visual pathway, receiving input from the optic tract.
labels.etymology: Latin 'genu' (knee), referring to its bent or flexed shape.

labels.description: A ridge of white matter connecting the optic tract and the superior colliculus.
The thalamic relay station for the auditory pathway, relaying to the temporal cortex.
A midbrain structure involved in visual reflexes, tracking, and gaze control.
A fiber bundle connecting the inferior colliculus to the medial geniculate body.
A midbrain structure that serves as a principal relay center for auditory information.
The primary output tract of the cerebellum, connecting it to the midbrain.
The largest of the peduncles, carrying information from the pons to the cerebellum.
A bundle of fibers connecting the medulla and spinal cord to the cerebellum.
Cranial nerve emerging from the lateral pontomedullary junction, controlling facial expression.
Sensory nerve for hearing and balance, emerging lateral to the facial nerve.
The medial dorsal column tract carrying fine touch and proprioception from the lower body.
The lateral dorsal column tract carrying sensory info from the upper body.
Motor nerve emerging from the lateral medulla/spinal cord to control neck muscles.
Motor nerve emerging from the pre-olivary sulcus of the medulla to control the tongue.
A mixed nerve providing autonomic control for thoracic and abdominal organs.
An oval mass in the medulla containing the inferior olivary nucleus, involved in motor learning.
labels.etymology: Latin 'oliva' (olive), due to its distinct shape.

labels.description: Ventral medullary structure containing the corticospinal (motor) tracts.
A nerve emerging from the lateral medulla involved in taste, swallowing, and sensation.
Motor nerve emerging from the pontomedullary junction for lateral eye movement.
Large nerve emerging from the lateral pons, responsible for facial sensation and mastication.
labels.etymology: Latin 'trigeminus' (triplets), for its three large divisions.

labels.description: The smallest cranial nerve, winding around the lateral midbrain to innervate the superior oblique muscle.
Nerve emerging from the interpeduncular fossa for most eye movements and pupil constriction.
Massive fiber bundles on the ventral aspect of the midbrain carrying descending tracts.
The sensory nerve transmitting visual information from the retina to the chiasm.
The continuation of the optic pathway from the chiasm to the lateral geniculate body.
The large dual-lobed mass of gray matter serving as the primary relay station for the cortex.
The most superior portion of the brainstem, including the colliculi and peduncles.
The bridge-like segment of the brainstem between the midbrain and medulla.
The lowest part of the brainstem, housing vital autonomic centers.
The bundle of nervous tissue extending inferiorly from the medulla.
A band of nerve fibers crossing the midline superior to the pineal gland.
A small endocrine gland producing melatonin, located posterior to the third ventricle.
The thick, rounded posterior end of the corpus callosum.
A short, thick vein formed by internal cerebral veins draining deep brain structures.
labels.etymology: Named after Galen, the Greco-Roman physician.

labels.description: A channel in the midbrain connecting the 3rd and 4th ventricles.
The most superior and anterior part of the cerebellar vermis.
A small lobe of the cerebellar vermis superior to the lingula.
The summit or highest part of the cerebellar vermis.
The sloping posterior section of the cerebellar vermis.
A leaf-like segment of the cerebellar vermis.
A lobule of the cerebellar vermis on the inferior surface.
A thin sheet of white matter forming the upper roof of the 4th ventricle.
A thin layer forming the lower roof of the 4th ventricle.
CSF-producing vascular tissue in the 4th ventricle.
A lobule of the cerebellar vermis located between the tuber and uvula.
A prominent lobule of the inferior cerebellar vermis.
The most anterior portion of the inferior cerebellar vermis.
The CSF-filled space running through the center of the spinal cord.
The crossing point of motor fibers in the lower medulla.
labels.etymology: Latin 'decussare' (to cross in an X shape).

labels.description: The central opening for CSF to exit the 4th ventricle.
A rounded lobule on the undersurface of each cerebellar hemisphere.
The lowest part of the brainstem continuous with the spinal cord.
The CSF-filled cavity between the pons/medulla and cerebellum.
A pathway coordinating eye and head movements.
The middle part of the brainstem.
Auditory reflex center in the dorsal midbrain.
Visual reflex center in the dorsal midbrain.
The motor nerve emerging from the interpeduncular fossa.
The structure where optic nerve fibers partially cross.
Small round bodies on the hypothalamus involved in memory.
White matter band crossing the midline at the midbrain-thalamus junction.
The anterior boundary of the 3rd ventricle.
A fiber bundle connecting the temporal lobes.
The primary sensory relay station.
Opening connecting lateral ventricles to the 3rd ventricle.
Vascular tissue in the 3rd ventricle roof producing CSF.
The central section of the fornix arch.
A bundle of nerve fibers connecting the two temporal lobes across the midline.
A C-shaped bundle of white matter fibers that acts as the major output pathway from the hippocampus.
A prominent gyrus on the medial surface of the hemisphere, involved in emotion and behavior.
The largest white matter structure in the brain, connecting the left and right cerebral hemispheres.
A grey matter cortical region of the brain that surrounds the hippocampus.
An area in the medial temporal lobe that functions as a hub in a widespread network for memory and navigation.
A compound structure including the hippocampus proper, dentate gyrus, and subiculum.
The hook-shaped anterior extremity of the parahippocampal gyrus.
An almond-shaped set of neurons deep in the temporal lobe, involved in processing emotions.
The anterior-most point of the temporal lobe.
Gyri on the inferior surface of the frontal lobe, resting on the orbital plate of the frontal bone.
The part of the frontal lobe that covers the insula.
A portion of the cerebral cortex folded deep within the lateral sulcus.
The part of the parietal lobe that forms the upper boundary of the lateral sulcus, covering the insula.
The deep lateral sulcus separating the frontal and parietal lobes from the temporal lobe.
Gyri found in the primary auditory cortex, located within the Sylvian fissure.
